museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: November makes 64-bit claims and 3D fighting visible.
museum.curatorNote: Initial researched draft for November 1993. Treat precise dates as source-noted; broad market atmosphere is marked as context.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched moments or context markers, with cautious wording where the month is a quiet drawer.

items.title: Atari Jaguar launches in North America in late 1993.
items.body: The launch is treated as a historical marker, with regional timing noted where the evidence is uneven.

items.title: UK buyers mostly watch the next generation from magazines.
items.body: From a UK perspective, this mattered through retail timing, import pages, playground talk and the monthly magazine cycle.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable games from the year, led by month-specific anchors when the evidence supports them.

items.title: Star Wars: X-Wing
items.body: LucasArts gives PC players cockpit drama and mission structure.
items.sourceNote: February 1993 release is commonly cited.

items.title: Star Fox / Starwing
items.body: Nintendo and Argonaut put polygon spectacle inside a cartridge.
items.sourceNote: Japanese/North American dates are early 1993; PAL title is Starwing.

items.title: Day of the Tentacle
items.body: LucasArts makes cartoon adventure timing feel effortless.
items.sourceNote: June 1993 release is commonly cited.

items.title: Secret of Mana
items.body: Square's action RPG becomes a multiplayer memory for many SNES owners.
items.sourceNote: Japanese and North American dates are 1993; exact PAL context differs.

items.title: Myst
items.body: Cyan turns CD-ROM into a quiet island of images, puzzles and atmosphere.
items.sourceNote: Myst source cites September 24, 1993.

items.title: FIFA International Soccer
items.body: EA begins a football series that will become part of annual sports culture.
items.sourceNote: 1993 release is secure; exact regional timing should be checked.

items.title: Doom
items.body: id Software releases the shareware episode that turns the PC into a social shockwave.
items.sourceNote: December 10, 1993 is widely cited.

items.title: Virtua Fighter
items.body: Sega's polygon fighters make 3D bodies feel like the next arcade language.
items.sourceNote: 1993 arcade release is secure; exact month depends on source.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Machines, formats and buying context around the exhibit month.

items.title: Mega-CD reaches Europe
items.body: Sega's CD add-on brings FMV, music and big promises into UK console magazines.
items.sourceNote: European Mega-CD release is cited in 1993 summaries; exact local retail timing should be checked.

items.title: 3DO launches in North America
items.body: The first expensive 32-bit living-room object makes the future look glossy but financially distant.
items.sourceNote: October 1993 North American launch context.

items.title: Atari Jaguar appears in the US
items.body: Atari's 64-bit claim adds another number to a market already drowning in hardware promises.
items.sourceNote: November 1993 US launch context.

items.title: PC CD-ROM begins to feel inevitable
items.body: Myst turns the CD drive into a cultural symbol rather than a luxury footnote.
items.sourceNote: Myst and multimedia PC context.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: How players found information before search, streams and social feeds.

items.title: Doom travels through shareware channels
items.body: The first episode spreads by upload, copied disk and office/school whisper network.

items.title: LAN play becomes a myth people can touch
items.body: Doom's multiplayer gives local networks a new social purpose.

items.title: The web is not yet the main games shelf
items.body: Online discovery is still FTP, BBS, Usenet and magazine addresses rather than search.

items.title: PC players start swapping config lore
items.body: Sound cards, memory managers and drivers become part of the ritual around playing.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A short atmospheric reading of the month from the player side of history.

items.title: A corridor could be a revolution
items.body: Doom made movement, sound and violence feel immediate in a way screenshots could barely explain.

items.title: CD-ROM looked adult
items.body: Myst's quiet images made games look like gallery objects as much as toys.

items.title: The future was fragmented
items.body: Mega-CD, 3DO, Jaguar and PC upgrades all claimed tomorrow, but none made choosing easy.

items.title: UK print got more serious
items.body: Edge made games feel like an industry, an art form and a consumer obsession at once.

Month-specific dates are used only where the reviewed sources support them; broader context is marked in certainty and sourceNote fields.
Magazine cover images are reconstructed placeholders and should be replaced with verified/licensed scans if used editorially.
